unit.title: What are the mechanisms of chemical change?

title: Electron sharing reactions
summary: Electron-sharing reactions are the world of the free radical: a species with a single unpaired electron that is fiercely reactive. This lesson builds the whole of R3.3 — homolytic versus heterolytic bond fission, how ultraviolet light generates radicals, and the free-radical substitution of alkanes worked all the way through its three stages of initiation, propagation and termination. We use the single-headed 'fish-hook' arrow to track single electrons, explain why a mixture of products always forms, and connect the chemistry to the real-world story of ozone depletion by CFCs. As with every lesson, the worked examples are laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between homolytic and heterolytic bond fission and the species each one produces.
Describe a free radical and explain the role of ultraviolet (UV) light in generating radicals.
Write the initiation, propagation and termination equations for the free-radical substitution of an alkane by a halogen.
Use the single-headed 'fish-hook' arrow to represent the movement of a single electron, and explain why a mixture of products forms.
Relate free-radical chemistry to real contexts such as the depletion of stratospheric ozone by CFCs.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Chain of Lonely Electrons
simpleExplanation.summary: Most bonds break by handing both shared electrons to one atom, making ions. But a bond can also split fairly, giving one electron to each atom. Each fragment is then left with a single unpaired electron — a free radical — and radicals are so reactive that they set off a chain reaction that keeps making more of themselves.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a shared bond as two people holding one rope between them. Heterolytic fission is one person snatching the whole rope and walking off (they become 'charged', an ion). Homolytic fission is the rope tearing exactly in half, so each person walks away holding a frayed end — an unpaired, reactive strand. Those frayed ends are radicals: they grab at anything to pair up, and in doing so they create a new frayed end somewhere else, which is why the reaction runs as a chain.
steps: A bond must first be broken evenly (homolytically) to make the first radicals — this needs energy, usually from UV light. This is INITIATION.
A radical attacks a stable molecule, makes a product, and generates a brand-new radical. The number of radicals is conserved, so the cycle repeats over and over. This is PROPAGATION.
Occasionally two radicals collide and pair up their lone electrons, forming a stable molecule and removing radicals from the mixture. This is TERMINATION.
Because radicals attack somewhat indiscriminately and can substitute more than once, the reaction produces a MIXTURE of products, not a single clean one.

flashcards.back: A species with an unpaired electron, shown with a dot (e.g. Cl•, •CH₃). The unpaired electron makes radicals extremely reactive. The dot is not optional — an examiner reads Cl and Cl• as different species.
A covalent bond breaks EVENLY: each atom takes one of the two shared electrons, producing two radicals. X–X → X• + X•. Favoured by non-polar bonds and driven by UV light or heat.
A covalent bond breaks UNEVENLY: one atom takes BOTH shared electrons, producing two oppositely charged ions. X–Y → X⁺ + Y⁻ (or X:⁻ + Y⁺). This makes ions, NOT radicals.

Ultraviolet photons supply the energy to break a bond homolytically and create the first radicals. UV is the condition that starts (initiates) free-radical substitution; without it the reaction of an alkane and a halogen is negligible in the dark.
The step that CREATES radicals from a stable molecule by homolytic fission, e.g. Cl₂ →(UV) 2Cl•. It happens once to get the chain going and is the only step that needs the UV input.
A step that USES a radical and MAKES a new radical, so the total number of radicals is unchanged. These steps carry the chain: e.g. Cl• + CH₄ → •CH₃ + HCl, then •CH₃ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + Cl•.
A step that DESTROYS radicals: two radicals combine, pairing their lone electrons to form a stable molecule, e.g. Cl• + Cl• → Cl₂ or •CH₃ + Cl• → CH₃Cl. This removes radicals and slows the reaction.

A half-arrow (⤙, one barb) that shows the movement of a SINGLE electron. A full double-headed curly arrow shows a PAIR of electrons. Radical mechanisms use fish-hooks because radicals move electrons one at a time.
An H atom on an alkane is replaced by a halogen atom via a radical chain (initiation → propagation → termination). Overall for the first substitution: CH₄ + Cl₂ →(UV) CH₃Cl + HCl.

flashcards.front: Why a mixture of products forms
flashcards.back: The chlorine radical can abstract H from a product as easily as from the starting alkane, so substitution repeats: CH₃Cl, CH₂Cl₂, CHCl₃, CCl₄. Termination also joins radicals (e.g. •CH₃ + •CH₃ → C₂H₆). One clean product is impossible.

UV light breaks the C–Cl bond in a CFC homolytically to give Cl• radicals in the stratosphere. Each Cl• catalyses the breakdown of many ozone (O₃) molecules in a chain, then is regenerated — the same radical-chain idea applied to the atmosphere.
Radicals carry a DOT and no charge; ions from heterolytic fission carry a CHARGE and no dot. Writing Cl⁻ where you mean Cl•, or dropping the dot on •CH₃, changes the species and loses the mark.

quickCheck.prompt: Which type of bond fission produces free radicals?
options: Heterolytic fission, because it makes charged ions
Homolytic fission, because each atom keeps one electron of the pair
Both produce radicals equally
Neither — radicals come only from ionisation
quickCheck.answer: Homolytic fission, because each atom keeps one electron of the pair

quickCheck.prompt: In the chlorination of methane, which step regenerates a chlorine radical so the chain continues?
options: Cl₂ → 2Cl• (initiation)
Cl• + CH₄ → •CH₃ + HCl (propagation)
•CH₃ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + Cl• (propagation)
Cl• + Cl• → Cl₂ (termination)

quickCheck.prompt: Which equation represents a termination step?
options: Cl₂ →(UV) 2Cl•
Cl• + CH₄ → •CH₃ + HCl
•CH₃ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + Cl•
•CH₃ + Cl• → CH₃Cl

quickCheck.prompt: Why does the reaction of methane with excess chlorine give a mixture of products?
options: Because chlorine radicals also substitute H atoms in the products, giving CH₂Cl₂, CHCl₃ and CCl₄
Because heterolytic fission makes several ions
Because UV light changes the molar mass of methane
Because termination is impossible with chlorine
quickCheck.answer: Because chlorine radicals also substitute H atoms in the products, giving CH₂Cl₂, CHCl₃ and CCl₄

quickCheck.prompt: What is the role of UV light in the mechanism?
options: It supplies energy to break Cl₂ homolytically and create the first radicals (initiation)
It joins two radicals together in termination
It converts CH₃Cl back into CH₄
It makes the propagation steps stop
quickCheck.answer: It supplies energy to break Cl₂ homolytically and create the first radicals (initiation)

faq.q: How do I decide whether a bond will break homolytically or heterolytically?
faq.a: It depends on the bond and the conditions. Non-polar bonds between identical or similar atoms (Cl–Cl, C–H, C–C) tend to break homolytically into radicals, especially when energised by UV light or high temperature in the gas phase. Polar bonds (C–Br, C–OH under acid/base conditions, and most reactions in solution) tend to break heterolytically into ions, because one atom is already electron-rich. Free-radical substitution of alkanes is the classic homolytic, UV-driven, gas-phase case.

faq.q: What is the difference between a fish-hook arrow and a normal curly arrow?
faq.a: A normal curly arrow has a full (double-barbed) head and shows a PAIR of electrons moving together — this is used in ionic (heterolytic) mechanisms. A fish-hook arrow has a single barb (a half-head) and shows just ONE electron moving. Radical mechanisms use fish-hooks because a radical has a single unpaired electron and rearranges electrons one at a time. You are not required to draw full mechanisms at this level, but you should recognise that the single-headed arrow represents single-electron movement.

faq.q: Why can't the reaction just stop after making CH₃Cl?
faq.a: Because the chlorine radical is not selective. Once CH₃Cl has formed, a Cl• radical can abstract a hydrogen atom from CH₃Cl just as readily as from CH₄, producing •CH₂Cl, which then reacts with Cl₂ to give CH₂Cl₂ — and the same logic continues to CHCl₃ and CCl₄. So with excess chlorine you always get a mixture. Using a large excess of methane instead of chlorine favours the mono-substituted product CH₃Cl, but never gives it perfectly pure.

faq.q: How does this alkane chemistry connect to ozone depletion?
faq.a: It is the same mechanism on an atmospheric scale. High-energy UV light in the stratosphere breaks a C–Cl bond in a chlorofluorocarbon (CFC) homolytically, releasing a chlorine radical, Cl•. That radical reacts with ozone (Cl• + O₃ → ClO• + O₂), and a further step regenerates Cl• (ClO• + O → Cl• + O₂), so a single chlorine radical destroys ozone catalytically — cycling many times, exactly like a propagation chain. This is why radical chemistry matters far beyond a lab flask.

sections.content: Not every reaction moves electrons in pairs. In this lesson we meet the free radical — a species carrying a single, unpaired electron — and the sharing reactions it drives. Radicals are made when a bond breaks fairly, giving one electron to each atom, and they are so reactive that they set off self-sustaining chains. We will build the free-radical substitution of alkanes stage by stage, learn how UV light and the single-headed 'fish-hook' arrow fit in, understand why these reactions never give one clean product, and see the same chemistry at work in the destruction of the ozone layer. Every worked example is written out the way our marking engine awards the marks.
Two ways to break a bond: homolytic vs heterolytic fission

A covalent bond is a shared pair of electrons. When the bond breaks, those two electrons have to go somewhere, and there are exactly two possibilities. In HETEROLYTIC fission the bond breaks unevenly: one atom keeps BOTH electrons, so it becomes a negative ion, while the other atom leaves with none and becomes a positive ion. In HOMOLYTIC fission the bond breaks evenly: each atom keeps ONE of the shared electrons, so each fragment leaves with a single unpaired electron. A fragment with an unpaired electron is a free radical, written with a dot, such as Cl• or •CH₃.

items: **Heterolytic fission:** X–Y → X⁺ + :Y⁻. Both electrons go to one atom → two IONS. Common for polar bonds and reactions in solution.
**Homolytic fission:** X–X → X• + X•. One electron to each atom → two RADICALS. Common for non-polar bonds energised by UV light or heat.
A **free radical** has an unpaired electron (shown by a dot) and is highly reactive; it seeks another electron to pair with.
Radicals carry a DOT and no charge; ions carry a CHARGE and no dot. Keep the two notations strictly apart.

sections.question: For each process, state whether the bond fission is homolytic or heterolytic, and identify the species produced. (a) Cl₂ → 2Cl.  (b) H–Br → H + Br. [2]
sections.solution:
**(a)** The Cl–Cl bond splits so that each chlorine atom keeps one electron, giving two identical fragments each with an unpaired electron: **2Cl•**. Because the electrons are shared out evenly and radicals are formed, this is **homolytic fission**.  [1]

**(b)** When H–Br breaks heterolytically, the more electronegative bromine takes both bonding electrons, giving **H⁺ and Br⁻** (ions, not radicals). Because the electron pair goes to one atom to form ions, this is **heterolytic fission**.  [1]

*Notice how the answer to (a) needs the dots and the answer to (b) needs the charges — mixing them up would change the species.*

sections.content: Free radicals and the role of UV light

Making the first radicals costs energy: you have to break a bond homolytically, and that does not happen on its own at room temperature in the dark. The energy is supplied by ULTRAVIOLET (UV) light. A UV photon carries enough energy to break a relatively weak, non-polar bond such as Cl–Cl evenly, generating two chlorine radicals. This is why the condition attached to free-radical substitution is always UV light (sometimes written as 'sunlight' or hv). Alkanes and halogens can be mixed in the dark almost indefinitely; shine UV on them and the chain reaction begins.

items: UV light provides the energy to break a bond **homolytically** and create radicals.
The condition 'UV light' (or hv / sunlight) is required and must be stated in the initiation step.
Only the initiation step needs the UV input; once radicals exist, the propagation chain sustains itself.
The single-headed **fish-hook arrow** represents the movement of ONE electron (a full curly arrow represents a pair).

sections.content: The reaction of an alkane with a halogen in UV light replaces a hydrogen atom with a halogen atom — a substitution — and it proceeds as a radical chain in three named stages. We use methane and chlorine as the standard example. The overall reaction for the first substitution is CH₄ + Cl₂ →(UV) CH₃Cl + HCl, but that overall equation hides the mechanism, which is what examiners test.

items: **Initiation** — radicals are CREATED by homolytic fission of the halogen using UV light:  Cl₂ →(UV) 2Cl•.
**Propagation** — the chain-carrying steps; each USES one radical and MAKES another, so the radical count stays constant:  Cl• + CH₄ → •CH₃ + HCl,  then  •CH₃ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + Cl•.
**Termination** — radicals are DESTROYED when two of them combine, pairing their lone electrons:  Cl• + Cl• → Cl₂,  •CH₃ + Cl• → CH₃Cl,  •CH₃ + •CH₃ → C₂H₆.
Each single electron shifting in these steps is drawn with a **fish-hook (single-headed) arrow**.

sections.content: Read the propagation steps carefully: the first one consumes a Cl• radical but produces a •CH₃ radical; the second consumes the •CH₃ radical but produces a Cl• radical again. The chlorine radical is regenerated, so the two steps cycle round and round, converting many molecules of methane and chlorine for every radical made in initiation. That is what 'chain reaction' means.
Why a mixture of products forms

Free-radical substitution is not clean. The chlorine radical is indiscriminate: it will abstract a hydrogen atom from a product molecule just as happily as from methane itself. So once CH₃Cl has formed, a Cl• radical can turn it into •CH₂Cl, which reacts with chlorine to give CH₂Cl₂ — and the process repeats to give CHCl₃ and finally CCl₄. On top of this, termination joins radicals in several combinations, so even ethane (C₂H₆, from two methyl radicals) appears. The result is always a MIXTURE, and this is a key limitation of the reaction. Using a large excess of the alkane favours the mono-substituted product but never delivers it pure.

items: Chlorine radicals substitute repeatedly: CH₄ → CH₃Cl → CH₂Cl₂ → CHCl₃ → CCl₄.
Termination gives extra products, including ethane from •CH₃ + •CH₃ → C₂H₆.
A single pure product is not obtainable; excess alkane only shifts the balance toward mono-substitution.

sections.question: Ethane, C₂H₆, reacts with chlorine, Cl₂, in the presence of UV light. Write one equation for each of the initiation, propagation (two steps) and termination stages of the free-radical substitution, using ethyl radicals where appropriate. [5]
sections.solution:
**Initiation** — homolytic fission of chlorine by UV light:
$$\text{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{\ UV\ } 2\text{Cl}\bullet$$  [1]

**Propagation step 1** — a chlorine radical abstracts a hydrogen atom from ethane, making an ethyl radical and HCl:
$$\text{Cl}\bullet + \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \bullet\text{C}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{HCl}$$  [1]

**Propagation step 2** — the ethyl radical reacts with a chlorine molecule, forming chloroethane and regenerating a chlorine radical:
$$\bullet\text{C}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}\bullet$$  [1]

**Termination** — any two radicals combine; for example:
$$\bullet\text{C}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{Cl}\bullet \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Cl}$$
(Also acceptable: $\text{Cl}\bullet + \text{Cl}\bullet \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2$ or $\bullet\text{C}_2\text{H}_5 + \bullet\text{C}_2\text{H}_5 \rightarrow \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$.)  [1]

**Correct radical notation throughout** (every radical carries a dot; the two propagation steps together conserve radicals).  [1]

*The structure is identical to the methane case — only the organic fragment changes from methyl (•CH₃) to ethyl (•C₂H₅).*

sections.content: Relevance: CFCs and the ozone layer

Free-radical chains are not a laboratory curiosity — they shape the atmosphere. Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), once widely used as refrigerants and propellants, are so unreactive that they drift intact into the stratosphere. There the intense UV light breaks a C–Cl bond homolytically, releasing a chlorine radical, Cl•. That radical then destroys ozone in a propagation-like cycle: Cl• + O₃ → ClO• + O₂, followed by ClO• + O → Cl• + O₂. The chlorine radical is regenerated each cycle, so one Cl• can destroy thousands of ozone molecules before a termination step removes it. It is exactly the initiation–propagation logic of alkane halogenation, playing out on a planetary scale — and it is why CFCs were phased out under the Montreal Protocol.
Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Mechanism questions in Paper 2 are marked analytically: each mark is tied to one specific correct step or condition. A method mark (M) is earned for a chemically valid step in the right stage; an answer mark (A) is earned for the correct species — with the radical dots present. Error-carried-forward (ECF) means that if you wrote a slightly wrong radical earlier but then used it correctly in the next step, the follow-on mark can still be awarded. Study how each mark below is pinned to a single line.

sections.question: Write equations for the initiation and the two propagation steps in the free-radical substitution of methane by chlorine, and state the condition required. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**

**Initiation** (condition: **UV light**):
$$\text{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{\ UV\ } 2\text{Cl}\bullet$$

**Propagation step 1:**
$$\text{Cl}\bullet + \text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \bullet\text{CH}_3 + \text{HCl}$$

**Propagation step 2:**
$$\bullet\text{CH}_3 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}\bullet$$

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **Mark 1 — initiation equation (M for a valid homolytic step, A for the correct radicals).** Awarded for Cl₂ → 2Cl• showing the halogen molecule splitting homolytically into TWO chlorine radicals. The engine requires the radical dot: '2Cl' or 'Cl⁻' does not score, because it is not the radical species.
- **Mark 2 — first propagation step.** Awarded for Cl• + CH₄ → •CH₃ + HCl. The method mark recognises a radical abstracting a hydrogen atom; the answer element requires the correct products, including the methyl radical •CH₃ with its dot.
- **Mark 3 — second propagation step.** Awarded for •CH₃ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + Cl•. **ECF applies:** a student who wrote a slightly different (but reasonable) organic radical in step 1 and then used that same radical consistently here can still earn this mark, because the credited idea is the radical reacting with Cl₂ to regenerate a chlorine radical.
- **Mark 4 — the condition.** Awarded for stating **UV light** (accept 'ultraviolet light', 'sunlight', or 'hv'). This is a standalone mark and is lost if the condition is omitted, even when all three equations are perfect.

**'Accept equivalents.'** The engine accepts hv, sunlight or 'ultraviolet' for the condition, and accepts the propagation steps written in either order. It does NOT credit an ionic step (e.g. Cl• + CH₄ → CH₃⁺ + ...): free-radical substitution is a radical mechanism, so ionic products score zero even if the atoms balance.

**Bottom line:** every dot and the stated condition are load-bearing. Three of the four marks are equations that must show radicals; the fourth is the UV condition. Omitting the condition or dropping a dot is the most common way this 4-mark question loses marks.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR mechanism the same way — step by step, with the radical dots and the condition each carrying their own mark, and ECF protecting a follow-on step built on an earlier slip. So always write the dots, write BOTH propagation steps, and never forget to state 'UV light'. An equation that is chemically ionic will score zero however neatly it balances.

items: **Confusing homolytic and heterolytic fission** — homolytic (even split) makes radicals with dots; heterolytic (uneven split) makes ions with charges. Don't describe a radical reaction as making ions.
**Dropping the radical dot** — Cl and Cl•, or CH₃ and •CH₃, are different species. A missing dot changes the species and loses the answer mark.
**Writing an ionic step in a radical mechanism** — free-radical substitution never has a step like Cl• + CH₄ → CH₃⁺ + ...; ionic products score zero even when balanced.
**Forgetting to state the UV condition** — the initiation step is meaningless without it, and it is a mark in its own right.

**Mislabelling the stages** — initiation CREATES radicals (needs UV), propagation USES and REMAKES a radical (chain-carrying), termination DESTROYS radicals (two combine). Putting a step in the wrong stage loses the mark.
**Claiming a single pure product** — chlorine radicals substitute repeatedly and termination joins radicals, so a MIXTURE (CH₃Cl, CH₂Cl₂, CHCl₃, CCl₄, plus C₂H₆) always forms.
**Not balancing HCl in propagation** — the hydrogen abstracted from the alkane must appear as HCl, not as a free H atom or as H₂.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 mechanism marked: write the initiation, propagation and termination steps for a free-radical substitution with full radical notation

items.label: IB Chemistry Data Booklet (bond enthalpies, Section 12)
